tldr: Part IV of a six-paper condensed-mathematics research program on topological phases of matter. It proves that stacking makes the set of gapped lattice phases a commutative monoid whose short-range-entangled (invertible) elements group-complete into a connective spectrum IP^cond_{d,G}, and poses -- as an explicit, unproven organizing conjecture (IV-2) -- that this microscopic spectrum agrees with the Freed-Hopkins bordism classification of invertible effective field theories. Evidence for the conjecture includes a genuine theorem in one spatial dimension (via Ogata's operator-algebraic index) and the free-fermion tenfold way, both checked by accompanying Haskell/QuickCheck code.
plain_language_summary:
Physicists classify gapped quantum many-body systems (systems with an energy gap protecting a unique ground state, like insulators) into 'topological phases': two systems belong to the same phase if one can be continuously deformed into the other without ever closing the gap. This paper is the fourth installment of a six-part mathematical research program that re-derives and reorganizes this classification using 'condensed mathematics,' a modern framework (due to Clausen and Scholze) built to handle continuously varying families, profinite limits, and disorder more cleanly than ordinary topology does.

The concrete new content concerns an operation called 'stacking': placing two lattice systems side by side without coupling them. The paper proves this operation turns the collection of phases into a commutative monoid (an algebraic structure like the whole numbers under addition), with a distinguished 'trivial' phase as the identity element and the invertible elements being exactly the 'short-range-entangled' (SRE) phases -- those with no exotic long-range quantum order. Standard algebra (Grothendieck group completion) then turns the invertible phases into an abelian group, and -- given one extra hypothesis about higher categorical structure inherited from an earlier paper in the series -- into a richer object called a spectrum, IP^cond, that also remembers families and deformations, not just which phases exist.

Separately, physicists have long classified the low-energy effective field theories that these systems flow to, using bordism theory and a different piece of mathematics (Freed-Hopkins theory). The paper's central claim -- clearly labeled as an open conjecture, not a proven result -- is that these two classifications, one built from microscopic lattices and one from field theory, actually agree. The authors split this claim into three versions of increasing strength (do the sets of phases agree? do the whole spectra agree? does the agreement hold for continuous families?) and are explicit that only the weakest version is established, and only in two special cases: one-dimensional spin chains (a genuine theorem, via an existing result of Ogata) and free-fermion systems in any dimension (via the well-known 'tenfold way' periodic table of topological insulators/superconductors, whose periodicity properties are checked by accompanying Haskell code). The paper is candid throughout about what remains conjectural and what noninvertible topological order is left out entirely.
key_contributions: Proves that the stacking operation makes the set of phases Phases_{d,G} into a commutative monoid, with the trivial product state as the unit and the short-range-entangled (SRE) phases as exactly the invertible elements (Prop. 'monoid', Prop. 'sre').

Gives a complete, self-contained proof of the universal property of Grothendieck group completion and isolates the 'stabilization element' as the precise algebraic shadow of physical stabilization by trivial ancillas, illustrated with a worked non-cancellative toy example (Thm. 'groth', Prop. 'stable', Ex. 'noncanc').

Constructs, conditional on a higher-categorical hypothesis inherited from Part I of the series, the invertible condensed phase spectrum IP^cond_{d,G} via the recognition principle for grouplike E-infinity spaces, and anchors it to Kubota's independently rigorous operator-algebraic Omega-spectrum (Thm. 'recognition', Thm. 'kubota').

States the central lattice-to-effective-field-theory comparison as an explicit three-level conjecture (IV-2: agreement of phase sets, of spectra, and of families over condensed probes) against the Freed-Hopkins bordism/Anderson-dual classification, rather than collapsing these distinct strengths into a single claim.

Assembles the concrete evidence bearing on the conjecture: a genuine theorem at the level of phase sets for one-dimensional spin chains (via Ogata's operator-algebraic index) and for free-fermion systems in every dimension (the tenfold way and its Bott periodicities), the latter verified by accompanying Haskell/QuickCheck code that regenerates the periodic table and checks the monoid and universal-property laws.
audience: Mathematical physicists and mathematicians working on the classification of symmetry-protected/topological phases via operator algebras, K-theory, and homotopy theory (spectra, E-infinity structures, bordism); most rewarding for readers already familiar with Parts I-III of this series, though the introduction, evidence section, and SSH worked example are largely self-contained for anyone with graduate-level background in either the condensed-matter or the field-theory side of the classification.
